diagrams.title: ESP Packet Structure (Header and Trailer)
diagrams.description: Complete visualization of an ESP packet showing all header fields, encrypted payload area, and trailer fields
elements: SPI (Security Parameters Index) - 32-bit field, shown as rectangular box with binary label
Sequence Number - 32-bit field, monotonic counter visualization
Payload Data - Large encrypted data block with lock icon indicating confidentiality
Padding - Small rectangular padding blocks (1-255 bytes) shown in different shade
Pad Length - 8-bit field indicating padding size
Next Header - 8-bit field showing protocol type (TCP=6, UDP=17, ICMP=1)
Authentication Data - Variable length ICV (Integrity Check Value) with checkmark icon
Arrows showing the boundary between encrypted and authenticated portions
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the complete ESP packet format, understanding which fields are encrypted (payload, padding, pad length, next header) and which are authenticated (SPI, sequence number, all encrypted fields). This is critical for understanding ESP's security properties.

diagrams.title: ESP Transport Mode vs Tunnel Mode
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison showing how ESP encapsulates packets in both modes
elements: Original IP Packet diagram with IP Header + TCP/UDP Header + Data
Transport Mode: Original IP header preserved, ESP header inserted after IP header, ESP trailer added, shows direct connection between two hosts
Tunnel Mode: Entire original IP packet encrypted, new outer IP header added, ESP header inserted between outer IP and encrypted payload, shows gateway-to-gateway or gateway-to-host communication
Arrows showing packet transformation steps in each mode
Labels showing what gets encrypted (shaded region) vs what gets authenticated in each mode
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the fundamental difference between transport and tunnel mode: transport mode preserves the original IP header (host-to-host), while tunnel mode encapsulates the entire original packet with a new outer header (gateway-to-gateway or for VPN functionality). Essential for exam questions on mode selection.

diagrams.title: ESP Processing Flow (Encryption and Decryption)
diagrams.description: Step-by-step flowchart showing ESP outbound and inbound processing
elements: Outbound Processing: Original IP packet → Select SA → Add ESP Header → Encrypt Payload + Trailer → Add Padding → Calculate ICV → Append Authentication Tag
Inbound Processing: Received ESP packet → Extract SPI → Lookup SA → Verify Authentication → Remove Auth Tag → Decrypt Payload → Remove Padding → Reassemble packet
Decision diamonds for replay attack check and integrity verification
Key exchange symbol (IKE) connecting to SA establishment
Error handling branches for failed authentication/decryption
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the complete ESP packet processing workflow. Understanding outbound (encryption + authentication) and inbound (verification + decryption) steps is crucial for troubleshooting and configuration. Aligns with university exam questions on ESP working.

diagrams.title: ESP in IPSec Architecture
diagrams.description: Position of ESP within the IPSec suite showing relationship with other protocols
elements: IPSec Architecture Box containing ESP and AH protocols
IKEv2 box on the left showing key management connection to ESP
Two modes (Transport and Tunnel) shown as branching paths from ESP
Encryption algorithms section: AES, 3DES, DES, RC5 shown with lock icons
Authentication algorithms section: HMAC-MD5, HMAC-SHA-1, SHA-2 shown with seal icons
SA (Security Association) database shown as storage component
IP Header and Upper Layer Protocols shown below as what ESP protects
diagrams.purpose: Students understand ESP's position in the broader IPSec framework, its relationship with IKE for key management, and the algorithms it supports. Provides context for how ESP integrates with other security components.

diagrams.title: ESP Security Services
diagrams.description: Visual representation of confidentiality, integrity, and anti-replay services provided by ESP
elements: Confidentiality shield icon with encryption algorithms listed (AES-GCM, AES-CBC, 3DES-CBC)
Integrity lock icon with authentication algorithms listed (HMAC-SHA-256, HMAC-SHA-1, HMAC-MD5)
Anti-replay window diagram showing sequence number tracking and duplicate packet rejection
Authentication scope indicator showing which fields are covered by ICV
Optional vs Mandatory service indicators for each security function
diagrams.purpose: Students learn that ESP provides confidentiality (encryption), integrity (authentication), and anti-replay protection. Important distinction: ESP with null encryption provides only authentication, while ESP with authentication provides integrity. Understanding which fields are covered by authentication is key for exam success.

charts.title: ESP Transport Mode vs Tunnel Mode Comparison
charts.content: Comparison table with columns: Aspect, Transport Mode, Tunnel Mode | Rows: IP Header, Use Case, Encryption Scope, Performance, Typical Application, NAT Traversal | Transport Mode: Original IP header preserved, Host-to-host communication, Original IP header + payload encrypted, Faster (less overhead), Remote access, VPN | Tunnel Mode: New outer IP header added, Gateway-to-gateway or gateway-to-host, Entire original packet encrypted, Slightly slower, Site-to-site VPN, Firewall traversal | This comparison is frequently tested in university exams.

charts.title: ESP vs AH (Authentication Header)
charts.content: Comparison table: Feature, ESP, AH | Confidentiality: ESP provides (with encryption), AH does not | Integrity: Both provide (ESP encrypts payload, AH authenticates entire packet) | Authentication: Both provide | Anti-replay: Both provide | Transport Mode: Both support | Tunnel Mode: Both support | NAT Compatibility: ESP (with NAT-T), AH (problematic) | Use Case: ESP for confidentiality + integrity, AH for integrity only scenarios | This table helps students understand when to use ESP vs AH.

charts.title: ESP Algorithm Classification
charts.content: Classification tree: ESP Algorithms → Encryption Algorithms → Block Ciphers (AES-CBC, 3DES-CBC, DES-CBC, CAST-128, Blowfish), Stream Ciphers (AES-CTR, AES-GCM) → Authentication Algorithms → HMAC-MD5 (128-bit), HMAC-SHA-1 (160-bit), HMAC-SHA-256, HMAC-SHA-384, HMAC-SHA-512 | Combined Modes: AES-GCM (provides both encryption + authentication) | Note: AES-GCM is recommended for new implementations.

charts.title: ESP Header Field Processing Order
charts.content: Step-by-step flowchart: 1. SPI (32 bits) - Identifies SA | 2. Sequence Number (32 bits) - Anti-replay counter | 3. Payload Data (variable) - Original data encrypted | 4. Padding (0-255 bytes) - Block alignment | 5. Pad Length (8 bits) - Padding size | 6. Next Header (8 bits) - Protocol number | 7. Authentication Data (variable) - ICV | Flow shows fields in order of appearance in packet, with encryption boundary marked between sequence number and payload data.

branches: Protocol Functions: Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Anti-replay
Packet Format: SPI, Sequence Number, Payload, Padding, Pad Length, Next Header, Auth Data
Security Associations: Established via IKE, Stored in SAD, Indexed by SPI
Position in IPSec: Works with AH, Uses IKE for key management, Can use null algorithms
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all ESP concepts. Helps students recall the complete picture: ESP provides confidentiality through encryption, integrity through authentication, supports two modes of operation, has specific packet format, uses various algorithms, and integrates with IKE for key management. This mind map serves as a quick revision tool before exams.

branches: Outbound Processing: Create ESP header → Add sequence number → Encrypt payload + trailer → Add padding → Calculate ICV → Append authentication data
Inbound Processing: Extract SPI → Lookup SA → Verify authentication → Remove authentication → Decrypt payload → Remove padding → Reassemble packet
Replay Protection: Sequence number incremented, Sliding window for verification, Reject duplicate packets
Key Management: IKEv2 negotiates algorithms, Creates SA with lifetime, Exchanges session keys
Error Conditions: Authentication failure (discard), Decryption failure (discard), Replay attack (discard), Sequence number overflow (rekey)
mindMaps.purpose: Detailed mind map for understanding ESP packet processing workflow. Essential for exam questions on how ESP processes packets at sender and receiver. The replay protection and error handling branches are particularly important for practical understanding and troubleshooting scenarios.
